fields.name: 15. Promoting Gender Equality
fields.content-01:
1. Problem-oriented Introduction: Gender Inequality (10 minutes)

‍

  • Teacher presents short scenarios of gender inequality (e.g. wage gap, leadership positions, job security, work–life balance)
  • Students take part in a Think–Pair–Share activity:
    • Think: First reactions and feelings
    • Pair: Short discussion with a partner
    • Share: Key thoughts in plenary

Focus: Awareness of inequality and identification of key issues

‍

2. Exploration Phase: Researching Gender Inequality (15 minutes)

‍

  • Students work in small groups, each focusing on one aspect of gender inequality in business or employment (e.g. wage gap, job security, leadership, family and career compatibility)
  • Groups research:
    • The current situation
    • Causes of inequality
    • Possible solutions

Focus: Building knowledge and analysing real-world examples

‍

3. Group Presentations & Perspective-taking (15 minutes)

‍

  • Each group briefly presents their findings
  • A guided whole-class discussion follows
  • Students use role cards (e.g. employer, employee, male/female perspective) to evaluate the proposed solutions from different viewpoints

Focus: Evaluating solutions and understanding multiple perspectives

‍

4. Transition: Introducing the Women’s Quota (5 minutes)

‍

  • Teacher presents a current case of leadership inequality
  • Introduction of the central motion:
    “The women’s quota reduces inequality between men and women.”

Focus: Connecting general inequality to a concrete policy debate

‍

5. Argument Development: Pyramid Discussion Method (20 minutes)

‍

Students work for or against the motion using a structured pyramid method.

Step 1 – Individual work:

  • Each student writes down two arguments supporting their position

Step 2 – Pair work:

  • Students discuss their arguments with a partner
  • They agree on the three strongest arguments and write them on sticky notes

Step 3 – Group work:

  • Two pairs join to form a small group
  • Arguments are discussed, refined, and arranged on the pyramid worksheet from most to least relevant
  • Groups identify possible counterarguments and strategies to respond to them

Focus: Structuring arguments and anticipating counterarguments

‍

6. Discussion Phase: Talk Show Format (20 minutes)

‍

  • A talk show setting is used:
    • Three students from each side participate
    • One student acts as the host
  • The remaining students listen and complete guided feedback sheets
  • Discussion includes argument exchange, follow-up questions, and rebuttals

Focus: Spoken interaction, argumentation, and feedback skills

‍

7. Reflection & Personal Transfer (5 minutes)

‍

  • Individual written reflection:
    • What is one concrete thing you can do to help reduce gender inequality in your own life or future workplace?

Focus: Personal responsibility and reflection

‍

Assessment

‍

Formative Assessment

  • Participation in discussions and group work
  • Engagement during research and argument development

‍

Summative Assessment

‍

  • Group presentations: clarity, depth of analysis, and feasibility of solutions
  • Talk show participation: argument structure, language use, and interaction
  • Feedback sheets: quality of peer feedback

Rubrics focus on creativity, critical thinking, depth of research, presentation quality, and reflection.
fields.content-02:
Lesson focus

‍

The lesson guides students from recognising gender inequality, through research and perspective-taking, to structured argumentation and reflection, using real-world examples and interactive discussion formats. The focus is on understanding inequality in the workplace and critically analysing the concept of a women’s quota.

‍

Objectives

‍

Subject-specific objectives

  • Understand the concept of gender equality and why it is essential
  • Identify key issues and challenges related to gender inequality
  • Analyse real-world examples of gender inequality and propose solutions
  • Critically examine the concept of the women’s quota
  • Reflect on personal beliefs and attitudes regarding gender equality

‍

Cross-disciplinary objectives

‍

  • Respond to counterarguments
  • Structure arguments clearly and logically
  • Provide targeted and constructive feedback

‍
fields.content-03:
Learning Outcomes

‍

‍By the end of the lesson, students will be able to:

  • Explain different forms of gender inequality, especially in the workplace
  • Use evidence to support arguments and evaluate proposed solutions
  • Recognise and articulate different perspectives on gender equality
  • Participate in structured discussions using appropriate language
  • Reflect on personal responsibility in promoting gender equality
fields.content-04:
Materials

‍

  • Appendix 1: Scenarios showcasing gender inequality
  • Appendix 2: Article 3 of the German Basic Law (Art. 3 GG)
  • Appendix 3: Role cards
  • Appendix 4: Current case of the women’s quota
  • Appendix 5: Pyramid worksheet and sticky notes
  • Appendix 6: Guided feedback sheets
  • Whiteboard / flipchart and markers
  • Digital devices for research
fields.content-05:
Tips for Teachers

‍

  • Clearly define roles within groups to ensure active participation
  • Provide scaffolding if needed (prepared materials, language support, argument templates)
  • Create a respectful classroom atmosphere for discussing sensitive topics

‍

Pedagogical Rationale

‍

The lesson follows a problem-oriented and discovery-based approach. Students first become aware of gender inequality through real-life scenarios, then build knowledge through research and discussion. Structured argumentation methods and role-based discussions help students analyse complex issues from multiple perspectives. The final reflection phase encourages students to transfer their learning to their own lives and future professional contexts.

Appendix 1

Source: eurostat, March 7, 2022, https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/de/web/products-eurostat-news/-/edn-20220307-2

Source: UK Time Use Survey, 2014-2015, https://www.researchgate.net/figure/The-gender-gap-in-time-use-among-parents-Unit-Daily-minutes_fig1_341851838

‍

Appendix 2

‍

Basic Law for the Federal Republic of Germany
Article 3

(1) All persons are equal before the law.

(2) Men and women shall have equal rights. The state shall promote the actual implementation of equal rights for women and men and take steps to eliminate existing disadvantages.

(3) No person shall be favored or disfavored because of sex, parentage, race, language, homeland and origin, faith, or religious or political opinions.

‍

Appendix 3

‍

ROLE CARDS – EXAMPLES

Role Card 1: Female Perspective
Name: Sarah Miller
Age: 32
Job Title: Senior Analyst at a Tech Company

Background: Sarah has been working in the tech industry for 7 years and has experienced the challenges of the gender pay gap firsthand. She is passionate about advocating for equal pay and believes that systemic changes are essential to address this issue.

Position on Gender Pay Gap: Sarah believes that the gender pay gap is a significant issue that requires immediate action. She views it as a result of both overt discrimination and unconscious biases that affect hiring and promotion practices.

Out of Sara’s perspective: evaluate and comment on the presented solutions to reduce gender pay gap.

‍

Role Card 2: Male Perspective
Name: David Thompson
Age: 40
Job Title: Human Resources Director at a Financial Firm

Background: David has over 15 years of experience in human resources and has been involved in implementing compensation policies within his organization. He is aware of the gender pay gap issue but believes that the reasons behind it are complex and multifaceted.

Position on Gender Pay Gap: David acknowledges that the gender pay gap exists but believes it is primarily influenced by factors such as career choices, negotiation practices, and work-life balance. He is open to discussing solutions but is cautious about measures that may unintentionally create division among employees.

Out of David’s perspective: evaluate and comment on the presented solutions to reduce gender pay gap.

‍

Appendix 4

‍

Current case: Womens Quota

A government introduces a rule: big companies must have at least 40% women on their boards by a deadline. Some companies support it because it forces fairer selection. Others oppose it because they say it is “not merit-based.”

Motion: “The women’s quota reduces inequality between men and women.”

3 affirmative arguments 

  1. Speeds up change when progress is slow.
  2. Reduces old “boys’ network” hiring.
  3. Creates role models and normalises women in leadership.

3  negation arguments

  1. Can create stigma (“she got the job because of the quota”).
  2. Doesn’t fix deeper causes (childcare, pay gap, career breaks).
  3. Can feel unfair to individuals competing for the same role.

1 quick question to start discussion: If the goal is equality, is a quota a fair tool or the wrong tool?

‍

Appendix 5

‍

‍

‍

‍

Appendix 6

‍

Class Discussion Feedback Sheet – Example

Date: ___________________
Topic: ___________________
Participants: ________________________________________________________

Please provide your feedback on the following aspects of the discussion:

Arguments
Were the arguments presented in a clear and logical manner?
Were the arguments sufficiently developed and supported with evidence?
Did participants use a consistent structure for their arguments (e.g., claim, evidence, reasoning)?

Flow of Discussion
Did the discussion progress smoothly from one point to another?
Were participants able to build on each other's ideas effectively?

Language
Did participants use appropriate language and terminology for the discussion?
Were phrases for debate and discussion effectively utilized?

Any additional comments or suggestions for improvement?

‍
